critique.summary: The article is a detailed tribute to Tony Hoare, highlighting his significant contributions to computer science, including the invention of the Quicksort algorithm, the development of axiomatic semantics (Hoare logic), and his work on concurrency with Communicating Sequential Processes (CSP). It also covers his influence on programming methodology, language design, and his role as a leader and catalyst in the field.
mainPoints: Tony Hoare invented the Quicksort algorithm, a highly efficient sorting method widely used today.
He developed axiomatic semantics, also known as Hoare logic, which formalized program correctness and influenced program verification.
Hoare contributed to programming language design, including Algol W and influenced Ada's concurrency features through CSP.
He was a key figure in promoting disciplined programming methodology alongside contemporaries like Dijkstra and Wirth.
Hoare introduced Communicating Sequential Processes (CSP), a foundational model for concurrent computation.
He played a major role in fostering formal methods research and mentoring many influential computer scientists.
Later in his career, Hoare advocated for pragmatic approaches to legacy code and continued active learning and research.
He initiated the Verified Software Grand Challenge to promote software correctness through formal verification.
critique.truthfulness: The article is accurate and well-supported by references to primary sources such as Hoare's original papers, lectures, and widely recognized contributions in computer science. The historical context and technical explanations align with established knowledge about Tony Hoare's work. The article also provides citations to authoritative publications and includes multiple perspectives on his influence and legacy, confirming the reliability of the claims.
considerations: The article is a tribute and thus emphasizes positive aspects of Hoare's career, with limited critical perspectives on his work or controversies.
It does not deeply explore the limitations or challenges of applying Hoare logic or CSP in modern software engineering practice.
The discussion of Hoare's 'billion-dollar mistake' regarding null pointers could include broader industry views on this issue.
The article is written by a single author with personal anecdotes, which may introduce subjective elements despite factual accuracy.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Tony Hoare's contributions to computer science, particularly his work on formal specification languages and the null pointer. Commenters reflect on the historical context, Hoare's influence, and the evolution of programming languages addressing null references.

comments.author: jgrahamc
comments.text:
In the early eighties, for example, he brought to Oxford the creators of two major formal specification languages: Cliff Jones with VDM, Jean-Raymond Abrial with Z. At Oxford, Z actually underwent a systematic rework, reminiscent of the Goethe quip reproduced above, with Frenchmen and mathematicians replaced by English mathematicians (or computer scientists). The new version enjoyed immense success in Britain, won a Queen’s Award and was used not only academically but in many mission-critical applications in industry, leading to a number of startups and work by such researchers (all having gone through Oxford at some point) as Jim Woodcock, Carroll Morgan, Jim Davies, J. Michael Spivey, Ian Hayes and Ib Holm Sørensen.

This was the world I walked into in 1986 as an undergraduate studying Mathematics and Computation. I was quite quickly indoctrinated in the ways of Z notation [1] and CSP [2] and had to learn to program in ML. I still have all the lecture and class notes and they are quite fascinating to look at so many years later. Funny to read the names of celebrated researchers that I just thought of as "the person who teachers subject X". I do recall Carroll Morgan's teaching being very entertaining and interesting. And I interacted quite a bit with Jim Davies, Jim Woodcock and Mike Spivey.

Having decided I wanted to stay and do a DPhil I managed to get through the interview with Tony Hoare (hardest question: "Where else have you applied to study?" answer: "Nowhere, I want to stay here") and that led to my DPhil being all CSP and occam [3]. I seem to remember we had an array of 16(?) transputers [4] that the university had managed to get because of a manufacturing problem (I think the dies were incorrecty placed making the pinouts weird, but someone had made a custom PCB for it).

Imagine my delight when Go came around and I got to see CSP in a new language.

[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Z_notation

[2] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Communicating_sequential_proce...

[3] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Occam_(programming_language)

[4] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transputer
comments.analysis: Provides a detailed, firsthand account of the academic environment influenced by Hoare, supported by references and personal anecdotes, making it highly credible and informative.

comments.author: DaleBiagio
comments.text:
Meyer makes an important point that often gets lost: the null pointer predates Hoare. NIL existed in McCarthy's Lisp in 1959, six years before Hoare added null references to ALGOL W. The "mistake," if it was one, was already widespread.

What made Hoare's 2009 confession so impactful wasn't that he was solely responsible — it's that he was the first person with that level of authority to publicly say "this was wrong."

That's what gave Rust, Swift, and Kotlin permission to design around it.
comments.analysis: Accurately contextualizes the history of null pointers and explains the impact of Hoare's 2009 confession on modern language design, showing nuanced understanding.

comments.author: mrkeen
comments.text:
> First, the null pointer is essentially inevitable if you want to model the world, which has references (things containing denotations of other things).

I took major exception to this. The real world doesn't have non-things, and references do not demand to refer to non-things.

If your domain does actually have the concept of null, just make a type for it. Then you won't accidentally use a 6 or a "foo" where a null was demanded.
comments.analysis: Offers a reasoned philosophical and practical perspective on null references, challenging common assumptions and proposing a sound alternative approach.

comments.author: tristramb
comments.text:
"To explain what I was doing in logic-driven software architecture I looked for a good metaphor and, on the spot, proposed that there was a kind of “contract” between caller and callee. He did not say anything, but his mere presence had enabled me to make my incipient ideas jell."

I hadn't realised that Hoare was present when Meyer first used the term 'contract' to describe his ideas.
comments.analysis: Provides an interesting anecdote linking Hoare to the conceptual development of design by contract, though it is more subjective and less verifiable.

comments.author: rswail
comments.text:
One of CS's heroes lauding another. I feel I know both author and subject better for reading this.

We are all very lucky to have lived through the foundation of a new science and new engineering over the last 50 years.
comments.analysis: A brief, appreciative comment that adds little factual content but reflects the community's respect for Hoare's legacy.

comments.author: hiccup
comments.text: Thank you for sharing this fantastic tribute
comments.analysis: A polite but minimal comment that does not contribute substantive analysis or information.

comments.text: saved for later. exactly the kind of deep dive i was looking for
comments.analysis: Expresses personal intent to revisit the content but lacks analytical or factual contribution.
